content:
12 min read
1 hour ago

--

Keeping a months-long refactor branch alive against a moving mainline is a reconnaissance problem, not a merge problem — and it survives on a ladder of oracles, not on trust.
Everyone knows the small merge. You pull mainline, git resolves ninety percent of it, you fix three conflicts by hand, you move on. That is not the problem this piece is about.
The problem is the volatile branch: a refactor so large it runs for months, moving, renaming, splitting, and reshaping code across hundreds of files, while the mainline it forked from never stops shipping underneath it. Let that branch drift and it dies — the divergence grows until no human, and certainly no language model, can hold it in their head. Keep it alive the naive way, by periodically opening a git merge and asking an AI to "sort out the conflicts," and you get something worse than a dead branch: a branch that compiles, looks merged, and has quietly dropped a mainline bugfix into a file the refactor moved out from under it.
We’ve been keeping exactly such a branch alive for months — an engine rebuild with a footprint of roughly 791 files — and it is still going. Over the eight reconciliation runs documented here, it has stayed healthy, and not because the model got smart enough to be trusted. It stays healthy because we stopped treating the merge as something the AI does and started treating it as something the AI scouts.
The earlier pieces in this series built the foundation: the Agentic Engineering manifesto for the mindset, the AGENTS.md filesystem piece for the mechanism, the how-to for the loop, and the legacy-refactor piece for the idea that you hand an agent an oracle it cannot argue with. This piece applies all of it to git’s worst case.
You might object that the real fix is to never be here — ship small, merge to trunk continuously, and a volatile branch never forms. Sometimes that is right. But some work has no safe incremental cutover. This branch is an R&D rebuild of a core engine: until the whole rebuild is coherent, no slice of it can go back to the mainline safely — not even periodically — without shipping a half-built engine to production.
The long-lived branch is not a process failure to be scolded out of existence; it is the honest shape of the work. Keeping it alive against a moving mainline is the price of doing that work at all, and the rest of this piece is how you pay it without the branch rotting underneath you.
Reconnaissance, not resolution
The reframe is the whole article, so here it is up front.
You do not ask the AI to merge the branch. You let git merge the branch. Git, with rename detection turned on, is extraordinarily good at this — it follows most of the refactor’s moves and renames, applies mainline’s untouched files cleanly, and auto-merges the many changes that happen to land on disjoint lines. The AI’s job is not to do that work. The AI’s job is reconnaissance: before you run the merge, produce a map of the small number of places where the merge will get it wrong, so you know exactly what to inspect afterward.
And the places where a merge gets it wrong are not random. On a big refactor they fall into exactly three shapes:

• Moved or renamed. Mainline changed a file the refactor relocated. The merge should carry the change to the new path — confirm that it did.

• Split or restructured. The refactor pulled one file’s logic into several. Mainline’s change has to be hand-ported into whichever new file now owns that behavior.

• Reshaped. The refactor changed a data shape — say, a wrapper shape we were flattening. Mainline’s change has to be re-expressed in the new shape.
Everything else — files only the mainline touched, files both sides touched on disjoint lines — the merge simply handles. So reconnaissance means reducing the entire divergence, which might be hundreds of files, down to the handful that match one of those three shapes. That is a problem an AI can actually reason about. “Resolve this branch” is not.
How agents fail a merge
The legacy-refactor piece named four ways an agent breaks a refactor. A volatile-branch merge stresses the same four, sharpened:

• Context blindness. Months of divergence does not fit in a context window. Hand the model a raw git merge mid-conflict and it sees a wall of markers with no idea which represent a real semantic collision and which are two disjoint edits git could have merged itself.

• The baseline trap. This one is specific to merging and it is lethal. Because the mainline flows into the branch on every reconcile, the branch already contains most of the mainline’s history. Diff the branch against the mainline tip and you get mostly false divergence — changes that are present on both sides. Reason from that and your entire map is garbage. An agent will make this mistake every single time unless the tooling makes it impossible.

• Plausible resolution. Asked to resolve a conflict, a model will confidently pick a side, or splice the two together into something that reads fine and silently drops a behavior. It looks done. It is not.

• Inventory drift. Both branches move between reconciles. Last run’s map is stale the moment mainline advances again. An agent has no instinct that its own notes have expired.
Every technique below answers one of these directly.
The harness: buckets, not diffs
The reconnaissance runs from a small, read-only Node harness (npm run reconcile). It touches nothing, runs no mutating git, and writes a set of plain-text inventory files. The single most important discipline it enforces is this: it never renders a diff body. It works from git diff --name-status, --stat, and per-file signature greps -- file names and change shapes, never the content. Pasting the diff is the tempting move, and it is exactly the move that overflows the context and invites plausible resolution. You reason over the inventory, not the code.
The harness recomputes the merge-base every run and sorts every changed file into four buckets:

• only-mine — the refactor’s own footprint. Context only; the merge does not touch these. (791 files, in our case — the scale of the thing.)

• only-sprint — files only the mainline changed. The merge brings these in cleanly; no action.

• both-touched — the same path changed on both sides. Most of these still auto-merge because the hunks are disjoint; only a few genuinely conflict.

• move-conflict — the mainline edited a path the refactor renamed away. The scariest bucket, because this is where a change can silently land at a dead path or get dropped.
That is the taxonomy, and it maps onto the three shapes from earlier: move-conflict is the “moved or renamed” case; the few both-touched files that truly conflict are where “split” and “reshaped” (and plain textual overlap) show up; only-mine and only-sprint need no reconciliation at all. A run reduces “hundreds of divergent files” to “791 for context, 136 that just arrive, 21 that overlap, of which 5 actually conflict, and 0 move-conflicts this time.” Now you have something to think about:
$ npm run reconcile
Reconciliation sweep: OLD=origin/sprint NEW=HEAD scope=.
Buckets:
only-mine (your refactor surface): 791
only-sprint (clean bring-forward): 136
both-touched (same path both sides): 21
move-conflicts (renamed away on NEW): 0
reshape candidates: 2Because the signature that classifies a reshaped file (the matcher for that wrapper shape, in our case) has to behave identically everywhere, it lives in the harness’s own code, not in a platform-dependent git regex. git grep is only a fast prefilter; the strict match is re-applied in the script. Small detail, but it is why the same file gets classified the same way on every machine.
The baseline trap, made concrete
The harness earns its keep on one line: it recomputes the merge-base each run rather than diffing against the mainline tip. Watch what happens across reconciles. Run 5’s merge lands and becomes part of the branch’s history. Run 6 recomputes the base and it has advanced — because the thing you merged last time is now behind you. Run 7’s merge lands; run 8’s base advances again. The fork point is a moving target, and anchoring on it is the difference between a true divergence map and a pile of false positives.
There is a continuity check riding alongside it. Midway through the project, the company’s git host changed and every commit hash in the repository was rewritten. Overnight, the SHAs from the first six reconciles no longer resolved to anything. The recovery was mechanical, not heroic: recompute the merge-base on the new hashes, then run git cherry to prove every mainline commit still showed as unmerged-and-pending -- none silently dropped, none re-surfaced across the rewrite. That check is the kind of thing a human does almost by reflex and an agent never thinks to do. It goes in the process so it happens every time.
Predict, then verify
Here is the loop that ties reconnaissance to the actual merge.
Before merging, we predict the exact set of files that will conflict — by simulating the three-way merge read-only (git merge-file --diff3 against the merge-base), not by guessing from overlapping line ranges. (This simulation is a manual read-only step, not something the harness does; the harness gives you the buckets, this tells you which of the overlaps will actually collide.) That produces a specific, falsifiable claim: "these five files will conflict, and no others."
Then you run the merge. And the payoff of the whole apparatus is a boring result. On our eighth run, the merge “produced exactly the five predicted unmerged paths — zero surprises.” That is what a working reconnaissance looks like: the map said five, the merge left five, and each one had a pre-written note on how to resolve it. When the prediction is wrong — run 6 turned up one conflict the map missed and one predicted conflict that auto-merged clean instead — that gap is the signal. A surprise conflict means your map was incomplete, and you learn why before it bites you.
This is the direct answer to plausible incompletion: the merge is not “done” because it looks done. It is done when it leaves exactly the set you predicted, and not one file more.
The ladder of oracles
The legacy-refactor piece argued you should hand the agent a mechanical oracle — compiler or tests — matched to how it is most likely to fail. A volatile-branch merge takes that further, because the failure can hide at several depths at once. You do not pick one oracle. You stack them, and each rung catches a class of failure the rung below it is blind to.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Rung one: the merge itself. Run it with rename detection (git merge -X find-renames=30%) and the move problem largely solves itself -- git follows the refactor's renames and places the mainline's change at the new path. A move it cannot follow surfaces as a conflict rather than a silent misplacement. The merge is the first oracle: it fails loudly on the moves it can't resolve.
Rung two: the three-way simulation. The read-only merge prediction, above. It catches the gap between what you think will collide and what actually will, before you commit anything.
Rung three: the compiler. A clean textual merge is not a correct merge. On one run, the branch had retired an old type in favor of a value-identical replacement, and a file the mainline added still imported the retired name in eight places. The merge was textually spotless — the two changes never touched the same lines — and the collision was real: a reference to a name that no longer resolves. That is exactly the class a type-checker exists to reject, and the team backs it with a pre-compile grep for the retired name rather than trust the merge’s clean bill. Textual cleanliness and semantic correctness are different properties, and the second one lives below the diff.
Rung four: the tests — and this is the one that matters. On our final run, the mainline added a couple of test spikes that
